title: Find Duplicates
description: This O(N²) duplicate finder is too slow. Which refactored version runs in O(N)?

options.A:
def find_duplicates(ledgers):
    return [x for x in ledgers if ledgers.count(x) > 1]

solution.explanation: Option B uses a set for O(1) lookups giving O(N) overall. A uses list.count() which is O(N) per call = O(N²). C sorts which is O(N log N). D gives wrong order.

hints: Track seen elements with O(1) lookup. Lists are O(N) to search.

title: Two Sum Optimization
description: This O(N²) function will timeout on large payloads. Which version correctly achieves O(N)?

solution.explanation: C uses HashSet for O(1) lookup per element = O(N). A is O(N log N) due to sort. B is O(N²). D has a bug — it doesn't check x != target-x when count is 1.

hints: For each element x, check if target - x was already seen in O(1).

title: Frequency Counter
description: This signal processor checks every pair to count frequencies — way too slow. Pick the O(N) fix.

solution.explanation: A uses unordered_map for O(1) average insertions/lookups = O(N) total. B is O(N log N) due to sort. C calls std::count per element = O(N²). D uses map which is O(N log N).

hints: A hash map counts all frequencies in a single pass.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: This brute-force window scanner is O(N·K). Which implementation correctly reduces it to O(N)?

options.A:
from heapq import nlargest
return [nlargest(1, stream[i:i+k])[0] for i in range(len(stream)-k+1)]
options.B:
result = []
for i in range(len(stream) - k + 1):
    result.append(sorted(stream[i:i+k])[-1])
return result

solution.explanation: D uses a monotonic deque — each element is added/removed at most once = O(N). A and B are still O(N·K). C uses a heap and is O(N log N) in worst case.

hints: A monotonic deque maintains candidates in decreasing order — front is always the max.

title: Anagram Detector
description: This checker compares every pair of strings — O(N²·L). Which version processes all strings in O(N·L)?

solution.explanation: B hashes each sorted string as a key — one pass O(N·L log L). C is still O(N²·L). A doesn't group correctly. D is correct but hard to read and has the same complexity as B.

hints: Sort each string's characters to get a canonical key, then group by that key in a HashMap.
